title: NG v. LM
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCivApp No. 211 of 2025
B E T W E E N
NG
Appellant
AND
LM
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
APPEARANCES: Mr. Robert Adams, KC, with Mr. Edward Marshall, II, and Ms.
Bryann Hepburn, Counsel for Intended Appellant
Mrs. Krystal Rolle, KC, with Mr. Darron Cash, Counsel for Intended
Respondent
DATES: 12 February; 21 April 2026
*******************************************
Civil Appeal – Discovery – Specific disclosure – Costs – Apportionment of costs – Exercise of
judicial discretion - Partial success - Rule 23 of the Matrimonial Causes Rules, Chapter 125
The Appellant (“the Husband”) filed a Summons pursuant to Rule 23 of Matrimonial Causes Rules,
Chapter 125, seeking specific disclosure of documents which was heard on 7 November 2024. On
22 January 2025, the learned judge refused the discovery sought in a written Ruling except for an
email the Respondent (“Wife”) was ordered to disclose.
On 14 May 2025, the learned judge ordered that the Wife recover 85% of her costs of the
application and that the Husband recover 15% of his costs, such costs to be taxed if not agreed. As
of the date of the submissions on costs, the Husband’s counsel stated that the Wife had still not
produced the documents ordered by the Court.
The Husband now appeals the costs order, challenging the propriety of the apportionment made
by the learned judge.
Held: Appeal dismissed. The Husband is to pay the costs of the appeal; such costs to be taxed if
not agreed.

It is clear that the learned Judge considered the Wife the successful party, but felt she should be
penalized for her unreasonable behavior. The result would have been the same whichever test the
Judge had elected to use, and as such, there is no basis for a finding that the learned Judge did not
exercise her discretion properly.
In considering this matter as a whole, it is clear that the Husband’s discovery application failed,
and that he could not be considered the successful party. This would be the case whether the test
being applied is the issue-based test or the principle of costs following the event espoused in Re
Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2).
In Re Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992] 1 W.L.R. 1207; applied.
____________________________________________________________________________
J U D G M E N T
____________________________________________________________________________
Judgment delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P:
INTRODUCTION
1. This is an appeal by the Appellant (“the Husband”) against the costs order made on 14 May
2025, following the determination of a discovery application. The Honourable Madam Justice
Hope Strachan (“the learned judge”) ordered that the Respondent (“the Wife”) recover 85% of
her costs of the application and that the Husband recover 15% of his costs, such costs to be
taxed if not agreed. The appeal challenges the propriety of that apportionment.
BACKGROUND
2. The application in the court below was made by the Husband by way of Summons filed on 21
October 2024 pursuant to Rule 23 of the Matrimonial Causes Rules, Chapter 125. The
Husband’s application was not successful, although he succeeded on one of the issues.
3. The Husband sought specific disclosure of certain documents from the Wife in relation to
allegations of abuse made towards her and the children of the family, A.D and G.G. The court
refused the application for discovery of the documents requested, except for the King’s College
email, which the court ruled that the Wife shall provide the email to the Husband.
4. At the end of the ruling, the court intended to award costs of the application to the Wife,
however, Counsel for the Husband objected. The objection was made on the basis that the
Husband was successful on one of the issues in dispute, in particular, the King’s College email,
and he should be awarded costs in respect of that issue. The Court, as a result, reserved its
decision regarding costs and granted leave to Counsel to make submissions within seven (7)
days. Both Counsel submitted written submissions to the court on 30 January 2025.
5. Counsel for the Wife submitted that the Wife had succeeded on the application and the
Husband had undoubtedly failed, and that there was, therefore, no basis for the Court to depart
2

from the usual rule that costs should follow the event and that such costs be awarded to the
Wife.
6. Counsel further contended that the application for discovery did not involve an argument on
the King’s College email and therefore, the Husband did not succeed on this application as the
Wife agreed to provide the documents, thus obviating the need for the Husband to advance the
application.
7. Counsel submitted that even if the King’s College emails were treated as a success, such
limited success in the context of the exceedingly wide nature of the requests was not sufficient
to deprive the Wife of the costs of the application, nor enable the Husband to recover costs
when the application has overwhelmingly failed.
8. Counsel for the Husband submitted that they sent a letter to Counsel for the Wife on 6 May
2024, inter alia, requesting copies of the correspondence between (i) the Wife and King’s
College and (ii) the Wife and Dr. Kerr which contained allegations of abuse against the
Husband by the Wife against the minor children of the family and herself.
9. The Wife chose not to respond to the Husband’s letter requesting documents and remained
silent while addressing other matters in this and other related actions. Consequently, some five
(5) months after the request for the documents was made, the Husband filed the Summons
seeking an order directing the Wife to produce, by way of specific disclosure, copies of the
documents he had requested as well as copies of other documents he reasonably believed to be
in the Wife’s possession, custody and power based on references made to such documents in
other correspondence he already possessed.
10. The Summons was heard by the learned judge on 7 November 2024. During that hearing, and
only after Counsel for the Husband had completed his oral submissions in support of the
summons, in reply, Counsel for the Wife conceded that the correspondence exchanged between
the Wife and King’s College containing allegations of abuse against the Husband was relevant
to the Husband’s allegations against the Wife and ought to be disclosed to him on that basis.
11. On 22 January 2025, the trial judge delivered her written Ruling to the parties, dismissing all
of the requests for discovery save the correspondence between the Wife and King’s College.
The Wife was directed to disclose the correspondence between her and King’s College to the
Husband, with the costs of and occasioned by the Summons to be determined.
12. As of the date of the submissions on costs, according to the Husband’s counsel, the Wife had
still not produced for the Husband’s inspection any of the documents the Wife had been ordered
to produce by the Court.
THE COURT’S FINDING
13. The essence of the learned judge’s findings is seen from paragraphs 25 to 29 as follows:
3

“[25.] The Court also considered the Court of Appeal decision
of Rawson McDonald v Paul R. Major SCCivApp No. 94 of
2017. Crane-Scott, JA made reference to the authority of
Scherer and another v. Counting instruments Ltd and another
[1986] 2 All ER 529 which Buckley LJ at page 537 derived ten
(10) guiding principles in relation to costs. The Court said:
“From the cases which we have cited and from Ottway v
Jones [1955] 2 All ER 585, [1955]1 1 WLR 706, Baylis
Baxter Ltd v Sabath [1958] 2 All ER 209, [1958] 1 WLR
529 and William C Parker Ltd v F J Ham & Son Ltd
[1972] 3 AIl ER 1051, [1972] 1 WLR 1583, which were
also referred to by counsel, we derive the following
propositions. (1) The normal rule is that costs follow the
event. That party who turns out to have unjustifiably
either brought another party before the court or given
another party cause to have recourse to the court to
obtain his rights is required to recompense that other
party in costs. But, (2) the judge has under s 50 of the
1925 Act an unlimited discretion to make what order as
to costs he considers that the justice of the case requires.
(3) Consequently, a successful party has a reasonable
expectation of obtaining an order for his costs to be paid
by the opposing party but has no right to such an order,
for it depends on the exercise of the court's discretion. (4)
This discretion is not one to be exercised arbitrarily: it
must be exercised judicially, that is to say in accordance
with established principles and in relation to the facts of
the case. (5) The discretion cannot be well exercised
unless there are relevant grounds for its exercise, for its
exercise without grounds cannot be a proper exercise of
the judge's function. (6) The grounds must be connected
with the case. This may extend to any matter relating to
the litigation and the parties’ conduct in it, and also to
the circumstances leading to the litigation, but no further.
(7) If no such ground exists for departing from the
normal rule, or if, although such grounds exist, the judge
is known to have acted not on any such ground but on
some extraneous ground, there has effectively been no
exercise of the discretion. (8) If a party invokes the
jurisdiction of the court to grant him some discretionary
relief and establishes the basic grounds therefor but the
relief sought is denied in the exercise of the discretion...
the opposing party may properly be ordered to pay his
costs. But where the party who invokes the court's
jurisdiction wholly fails to establish one or more of the
4

ingredients necessary to entitle him to the relief claimed,
whether discretionary or not, it is difficult to envisage a
ground on which the opposing party could properly be
ordered to pay his costs... (9) If a judge, having relevant
grounds on which to do so, has on those grounds, or some
of them, made an order as to costs in the exercise of his
discretion, his decision is final unless he gives leave to a
dissatisfied party to appeal. (10) If, however, he has made
his order having no relevant grounds available or having
in fact acted on extraneous grounds, this court can
entertain an appeal without leave and can make what
order it thinks fit.” [Emphasis added]
…
[26.] In considering the principles in Scherer v Counting, supra,
the Court accepts that the husband requested the email to
King’s College from the wife by a letter dated 6 May 2024. The
wife did not provide the email. Subsequently, the husband filed
the Summons dated 21 October 2024 for discovery. The Court
agrees with Counsel for the husband that it was only when
Counsel completed his oral submissions that Counsel for the
wife in her submissions agreed to provide the King’s College
email. Paragraph 22 of the ruling in relation to this point is
outlined below:
“22. In relation to the King's College email, there is a
reference in the Affidavit of the Applicant to
communication between the Respondent and King’s
College. Therefore, there is a basis to suspect that there
is communication that an email exists and it is not in the
Applicant’s possession. In the premises the necessary
foundation has been met in the Affidavit of the Applicant
as to the existence and relevance of this email to King's
College. Counsel for the Respondent has agreed to
provide this email to Counsel for the Applicant.”
[27.] In these circumstances, the wife’s failure to provide the
King’s College email that was in her possession to the husband
may have resulted in the application for discovery being made.
Notwithstanding the concession made by Counsel for the wife,
the Court cannot disregard the time and resources used by
Counsel for the husband to file his application. Whilst Counsel
was not successful on every issue, he did succeed on the King’s
College issue and as such he should be awarded costs in that
5

regard. The wife, however was successful on all other issues and
should be awarded her costs of these proceedings.
[28.] It has been brought to the Court’s attention, through the
husband’s submissions, that following the ruling that was
delivered in respect of this matter, the wife has not provided the
King’s College email to the husband. The Court’s ruling is not
to be ignored. The email is to be provided to the husband within
seven (7) days of this ruling.
CONCLUSION
[29.] In exercising my discretion, the court orders:
1. That the wife having succeeded in defending the
application for discovery of the husband on all of the
issues, except for disclosure of the document from King’s
College shall have Eighty-Five (85) % of her costs to be
taxed if not agreed.
2. That the husband having succeeded on the application
for discovery of the King’s College document shall have
Fifteen (15) % of his costs to be taxed if not agreed.”
LEAVE TO APPEAL FROM THE COURT BELOW
14. On 21 May 2025, the Husband filed his application before the learned judge for leave to appeal
the Ruling on costs in accordance with Rule 27(5) of the Court of Appeal Rules. He was granted
leave to appeal the Ruling on costs on 20 November 2025. The learned judge therefore took
about six (6) months to make her decision on that application for leave to appeal. It was
therefore necessary for the Husband to make an application for an extension of time in which
to file her appeal before this Court.
15. On 24 November 2025, a mere four (4) days after leave to appeal was granted, the Husband
filed his Notice of Appeal in this Court (albeit without leave to extend the time for appealing)
and served the same upon the Wife by their Counsel.
APPLICATION FOR EXTENSION OF TIME WITHIN WHICH TO FILE AN APPEAL
16. It was not until 19 January 2026 that the Husband made an application for an extension of the
time allotted to file his appeal. The application was supported by an Affidavit of the Husband
filed on the same date.
17. The application for the extension came on for hearing before us on 12 February 2026. At that
hearing, the extension of time was granted by agreement, and there was no order as to costs.
6

We ordered the parties to provide written submissions, and we indicated that we would deal
with the matter on the papers.
18. The Husband filed his submissions on 5 March 2026, and the Wife filed hers on 18 March
2026. However, also filed with the Wife’s submission was a Notice of Preliminary Objection
accompanied by an Affidavit sworn by Wallace I Rolle on behalf of the Wife. In his Affidavit,
Mr. Rolle asserted that the Notice of Motion filed by the Husband on 16 February 2026 was
inconsistent with the leave granted by the court below, as well as that sanctioned by this Court
in granting the extension of time.
19. Specifically, at paragraph 13 of his Affidavit, Mr. Rolle stated as follows:
“13. Specifically, the Draft Notice of Appeal attached to the
Appellant’s Summons seeking leave and exhibited to the
Affidavit seeking an extension of time at Paragraph (i) on Page
3 states, “...FOR AN ORDER: (i) Setting aside the Ruling on
Costs delivered by the Learned Judge in its entirety, or in
alternative, varied” while the Notice of Appeal filed by the
Appellant on 16th February, 2026 pursuant to such leave and
extension of time at Paragraph (i) on Page 3 states “FOR AN
ORDER: Setting aside the Ruling on Costs delivered by the
Learned Judge in its entirety and remitting back to the Learned
Judge for re-hearing, or in alternative, varied by setting aside
the order that the Appellant pay costs to the Respondent and
directing the Respondent to pay half of the Appellant's costs in
respect of the application before the Learned Judge in the court
below”; [Emphasis Added]” “[Emphasis Added]
20. We have received no response from the Husband relative to the Rolle Affidavit. However, it is
clear that the Notice filed on 16 February 2026 is inconsistent with the Notice approved by this
Court in granting the extension. At the hearing before us Mr. Adams, KC did indicate an
intention to expand on his submissions, but no leave was sought nor granted to alter or amend
his Notice of Appeal.
21. As a result, on the facts before us, we feel constrained to consider the appeal on the terms of
the Draft Notice exhibited to his application for the extension of time. I do not see that this
affects our ability to deal with this matter because if necessary, we are able to set aside or vary
the learned judge’s order and give any directions or make such order which we consider will
meet the justice of the case. In my view, the Wife is in no way prejudiced as the actual grounds
of appeal remain the same.
THE APPEAL
22. The grounds of appeal set out in the Notice of Motion are as follows:
7

“1. The Learned Judge erred in law and failed to apply the well
settled principles governing the exercise of the court’s discretion
respecting the award of costs in relation to a partially successful
party as determined and applied in Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992]
1 W.L.R. 1207 per Nourse J; namely, that (a) the general rule
regarding the award of costs does not cease to apply because the
successful party raises issues or makes allegations on which he
has failed and (b) a court ought only deprive a partially
successful party of its costs and/or order that party to pay the
whole or a part of the other party’s costs in circumstances where
the court has found the partially successful party raised issues
or made allegations ‘improperly’ or ‘unreasonably’.
2. The Learned Judge erred in law when she awarded eighty five
percent (85%) of the Respondent’s costs to be taxed if not agreed
in circumstances where no finding was made in the Ruling on
Costs that the issues raised by the Appellant in the Discovery
Application or the allegations made by him therein were either
improper or unreasonable.
3. The Learned Judge also erred in law when she awarded
fifteen percent (15%) of the Appellant's costs to be taxed if not
agreed based on her determination in paragraph 27 of the
Ruling on Costs that the court is unable to disregard the time
and resources used by Counsel for the Appellant to file the
Discovery Application in circumstances where it was also
determined in paragraph 26 of the Ruling on Costs that the
Discovery Application was necessitated by the Respondent’s
refusal to provide material correspondence she was obligated to
provide to the Appellant despite requests having been made for
that correspondence since 6 May 2024 and had only agreed to
provide the Kings College correspondence at the hearing of the
Discovery Application.
4. The Appellant hereby reserves the right to supplement or add
additional grounds of appeal to this Notice of Appeal.
SUBMISSIONS, ANALYSIS, AND FINDINGS
23. In my opinion, the grounds of appeal raise the sole issue of whether the learned judge erred in
principle, or otherwise exceeded the ambit of her discretion in apportioning the costs of the
discovery application as she did.
24. Mr. Adams, KC contended that the learned Judge erred in treating the matter as one appropriate
for an issue-based apportionment. He asserted that she was required to have recourse to the
8

Matrimonial Causes Rules, specifically Rule 62(3), which he contends governed the Learned
Judge’s decision on costs at that time.
25. He stated further that Rule 62 (3) of the Matrimonial Causes Rules provides that the costs
allowed to attorneys and taxation of such costs, shall be in accordance with the Rules of the
Supreme Court of England in force at the time, so far as the same are applicable. The Rules of
the Supreme Court of England in force in 1957 were the Annual Practice 1957 (“the 1957
Rules”). This position remained until 1 July 2025, when the Matrimonial Causes Rules were
amended and the Matrimonial Causes (Amendment) Rules 2025 came into effect. It was at that
time that the provisions of The Supreme Court Civil Procedure Rules, 2022 (“the CPR”)
became the default rules for all matrimonial causes, including the present one.
26. As a consequence, he concluded that it is manifest that when the Ruling on Costs was made
on 15 May 2025, the Learned Judge could not rely upon the costs procedure outlined in the
CPR and was bound to follow the costs procedure outlined in Rule 62(3) of the Matrimonial
Causes Rules.
27. The issue based test in the award of costs operates on the principle that costs should be
allocated between parties based on specific issues in the particular proceedings. As such where
a party has won overall on the case but has lost on one or more issues, the successful party is
entitled to his costs unless the Court is satisfied that the costs were materially increased by his
unreasonably raising certain issues.
28. Counsel further submitted that the principle in the case of In Re Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992]
1 WLR 1207 is what should have guided the Learned Judge. In the Elgindata case, the position
was explained by the English Court of Appeal as follows:
“I now come to Warner J's judgment on costs. Having referred
to the general rule that a successful plaintiff is entitled to his
costs notwithstanding that he may have failed on some issues, he
said that he had to look at the realities of the case.
…
In order to show that the judge erred I must state the principles
which ought to have been applied. They are mainly recognized
or provided for, it matters not which, by section 51 of the
Supreme Court Act 1981 and the relevant provisions of R.S.C.,
Ord. 62, in this case rules 2(4), 3(3) and 10. They do not in their
entirety depend on the express recognition or provision of the
rules. In part they depend on established practice or implication
from the rules. The principles are these. (i) Costs are in the
discretion of the court. (ii) They should follow the event, except
when it appears to the court that in the circumstances of the case
some other order should be made. (iii) The general rule does not
cease to apply simply because the successful party raises issues
9

or makes allegations on which he fails, but where that has caused
a significant increase in the length or cost of the proceedings he
may be deprived of the whole or a part of his costs. (iv) Where
the successful party raises issues or makes allegations
improperly or unreasonably, the court may not only deprive him
of his costs but may order him to pay the whole or a part of the
unsuccessful party's costs. Of these principles the first, second
and fourth are expressly recognized or provided for by rules
2(4), 3(3) and 10 respectively. The third depends on well-
established practice. Moreover, the fourth implies that a
successful party who neither improperly nor unreasonably
raises issues or makes allegations on which he fails ought not to
be ordered to pay any part of the unsuccessful party’s costs. It
was because of his disregard of that principle that the judge
erred in this case.” [Emphasis Added]
29. Mr. Adams contended that in this case, there is no finding in the Ruling on Costs that the
Husband misconducted himself in any way by making the discovery application, justifying the
departure from the general principle.
30. Mrs. Rolle, KC on behalf of the Wife, submitted that the issue as to the CPR does not arise
from any of the grounds in the Notice of Motion. However she further contends that having
regard to the fact that the Husband’s application for discovery related to a number of items
and only one was ordered by agreement, there is no reasonable basis on which it could be said
that the Husband was the successful party. She further noted that in any event he received costs
relative to his success on that one item. In these circumstances she concluded that there was
no basis to depart from the principle that costs follow the event.
31. In considering this matter as a whole, it is clear that the Husband’s discovery application failed
and in my view, he could not be considered the successful party. This would be the case whether
the test being applied is the issue-based test or the principle of costs following the event
espoused in Elgindata.
32. The Learned Judge was clear on the basis on which she awarded to the Husband 15% costs.
This is seen at paragraph 27 of her Judgment, where she stated as follows:
“27… the wife’s failure to provide the King’s College email that
was in her possession to the husband may have resulted in the
application for discovery being made. Notwithstanding the
concession made by Counsel for the wife, the Court cannot
disregard the time and resources used by Counsel for the
husband to file his application. Whilst Counsel was not
successful on every issue, he did succeed on the King’s College
issue and as such he should be awarded costs in that regard. The
wife, however was successful on all other issues and should be
awarded her costs of these proceedings.”
10

33. It is clear that the learned judge considered the Wife the successful party but felt she should be
penalized for her unreasonable behavior. In my view, the result would have been the same
whichever test the learned judge had elected to use. The Husband has failed to demonstrate
any basis upon which this Court could properly interfere with the learned judge’s exercise of
her discretion.
CONCLUSION
34. For the foregoing reasons, I would dismiss the appeal and order that the Husband pay the costs
of the appeal; such costs to be taxed if not agreed.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
35. I agree.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
36. I also agree.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
11
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas 

 
 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 Return to Main Page
summary: Civil Appeal – Discovery – Specific disclosure – Costs – Apportionment of costs – Exercise of judicial discretion - Partial success - Rule 23 of the Matrimonial Causes Rules, Chapter 125
